planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter while dormant (January–February in zone 9); Desirable requires a cross-pollinator such as Stuart, Elliott, or Caddo for reliable nut production since it is a Type II pollinator (pistillate flowers receptive before pollen shed). Space 50–60 feet apart for full-sized trees, or 30–35 feet for orchard plantings.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained, fertile bottomland-type soil (pH 6.0–7.0) in full sun; Desirable is the most widely planted named pecan cultivar in the United States and the benchmark against which other commercial varieties are measured. Developed by the USDA and released in 1940, it produces large, well-filled nuts (50–55 per pound) with an excellent kernel percentage (58–62%) and superb flavor — considered by many pecan growers to be the highest-quality commercial pecan available. It is a consistent, reliable producer in zone 7–9 and well-adapted to the Gulf Coast South from Texas to Georgia. The principal challenge is susceptibility to pecan scab disease (Fusicladium effusum) in humid climates — in very humid areas like coastal Louisiana and Mississippi, it may require fungicide protection in wet years. Space trees widely: pecans ultimately become very large trees (60–80 feet tall, 40–60 feet wide) that shade out everything below. Supplement with zinc spray in spring; pecan is notorious for zinc deficiency on alkaline soils.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — Desirable pecan release notes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Pecan Variety Selection (E-459)
LSU AgCenter — Commercial Pecan Production in Louisiana
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Pecan Varieties for Georgia

planting.note: Plant dormant bare-root trees in late winter (January–February in zone 9); Stuart is a Type II pollinator that pairs well with Desirable (Type I) for cross-pollination. Established in the landscape before 1874, Stuart is one of the oldest named pecan varieties still in commercial production.

directions: Plant in deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Stuart is an old heirloom pecan variety originating in the Gulf South before the Civil War and one of the most historically important named pecans — it was among the first varieties to be systematically propagated by grafting and was widely planted throughout the Southeast from the 1880s through mid-20th century. Nuts are very large (45–50 per pound) with an excellent kernel percentage, rich flavor, and attractive appearance that made Stuart the standard commercial variety for much of the 20th century. Though partially supplanted by more scab-resistant modern varieties in humid commercial orchards, Stuart remains extremely popular for home planting in zone 7–9 because of its large, beautiful nuts, adaptability to a wide range of soils, and long productive lifespan — well-established Stuart trees may continue producing heavily for 100+ years. The principal weakness is moderate scab susceptibility in very wet years; in most zone 9 home orchards, this is manageable without spraying. An excellent choice for the Gulf Coast gardener who wants large, flavorful nuts from a landscape tree.
sources: University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Pecan Production (B 1304)
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Pecan Variety Performance in Texas
LSU AgCenter Publication #1729 — Pecan Varieties for Home Orchards
L.J. Grauke, USDA Pecan Breeding Program, Somerville, TX

planting.note: Plant dormant bare-root trees January–February in zone 9; Elliott is a Type II pollinator and pairs best with Desirable (Type I) or Curtis (Type I) for cross-pollination. Its exceptional scab resistance makes it the recommended variety for humid coastal zone 8–9 areas where scab disease is a persistent problem.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained soil in full sun; Elliott is a Florida-origin pecan variety selected for commercial production in the humid Gulf Coast Southeast because of its outstanding resistance to pecan scab disease — the most economically damaging disease of pecans in the humid South. While Stuart and Desirable require protection in rainy years, Elliott produces consistent yields without fungicide programs in most Gulf Coast locations, making it the variety of choice for low-spray and organic home orchards in zone 8–9. Nuts are smaller than Desirable or Stuart (60–70 per pound) but kernel quality is excellent — thin shells, high kernel fill (55–60%), and very good flavor. Elliott is a late-season variety, maturing in October–November after most other varieties. Its smaller stature (60–70 feet mature) compared to some pecan giants also makes it slightly more manageable. An excellent choice for humid coastal and transitional zone 9 gardens where disease pressure is high.
sources: Florida Cooperative Extension Service — Pecan Production in Florida (Circular 1327)
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Disease-Resistant Pecan Varieties
LSU AgCenter — Pecan Scab Management and Resistant Varieties
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Pecan Variety Trials

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in late winter to early spring; Scuppernong is self-fertile but benefits from a second muscadine variety nearby for maximum berry set. Build a strong arbor or trellis before planting — mature Scuppernong vines are massive and heavy with fruit. In zone 9, plant February–March for maximum first-season growth.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun; Scuppernong is a bronze-skinned muscadine grape (Vitis rotundifolia) that is arguably the oldest cultivated grape in North America — the original 'Mother Vine' on Roanoke Island, North Carolina, is estimated to be over 400 years old and still produces fruit. It is the ancestral cultivar of the entire muscadine grape industry in the southeastern United States. Scuppernong grapes are large (1–1.5 inches), round, bronze-green to golden when ripe, with a distinctively musky, sweet, complex flavor very different from European grapes — they are used for fresh eating, wine, juice, and preserves. Muscadines are extremely well-adapted to the hot, humid Gulf South, where they naturally resist Pierce's disease, black rot, and other diseases that devastate Vitis vinifera grapes. Vines are vigorous and long-lived, requiring a strong overhead arbor or high-wire trellis. Harvest in late August–September when berries drop from the cluster at a touch. Prune heavily each winter, leaving only short spurs on the permanent arms.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Muscadine Grape Production (AG-94)
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Muscadine Grape Culture (B 1145)
LSU AgCenter — Muscadine Grape Production in Louisiana
Virginia Dare Winery and Roanoke Island historical records
